speaker: interviewer
text: If you will permit us to return to Auschwitz for a few more moments. If you could give us more of a feeling for who Dr. Mengele was, how he related to people, how he-- his attitude towards people that he dealt with.

speaker: interviewee
text: As-- as I remember Mengele, he was in his late 30s. And my contact with him was simply that I had received orders which were purely administrative. The man never moved an eye-- bat an eyelid. He didn't make any twitch, any move. He was a stony face. Many years later when I read the book on him-- I've seen the film, 'The Young Lions,' I'm sure that that was the type of a Nazi who was simply trained in medicine and who was trained in Nazi doctrine, and for whom the Jews were vermin. They simply had nothing to do with human beings. It was a sector of inhabitants of this earth which had to be exterminated. And he was just the grand exterminator at that point. And his function he did certainly very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his manner? What was his manner like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards-- let's say towards-- towards this-- this hospital block-- hospital compound. He was purely, I mean, strict orders. He listened to the report. He watched how person is showing a hypnosis, how it's done. He was curious enough to find out whether somebody really has got typhus or typhoid, or rather were-- there were all precautions done not to spread the disease. But [INAUDIBLE] just so far as I can say. And from our block, the next 20 minutes, one could see him standing on the ramp talking to the other SS men. Quite matter of fact dividing the vermin into usable and unusable. It was something what the German themselves consider themselves ubermenschen, over, one of the Superman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while the rest, they were not people at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did Dr. Mengele come to your hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: If one calls it a hospital. [LAUGHS] It's-- he came approximately once a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it always at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, yes. Around 9:00-ish or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the hospital routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was very little routine. Simply people were put there mostly when there was danger that they would infect other prisoners. So sometimes they were three to a-- a level of a bunk, or sometimes they were six, sometime they were seven. Sometimes there was only one, depending simply on how many people died and how many people were put in an [INAUDIBLE]. People were in hiding, for they were afraid to go to so-called hospital. For some people left, but very few back, I mean, into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the mortality rate in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Estimated 80%.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that there were sometimes up to eight people in a bunk, what was the width of this bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: One and a half meter. Two meters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight human beings who were very ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Lying like sardines in a-- in a box-- in a small sardine box.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sanitary conditions like in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were changed as much as one could. But let's say that there was a wave of dysentery, so it was catastrophic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you elaborate for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was seeping through. Let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cope with this as a medical person there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, luckily at that point I was very little medical person. But one could do [INAUDIBLE] simply try to take the sick and put them onto a different bed or different part of the bunk and clean it. Hose it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And most those who came to the hospital were suffering from malnutrition or other diseases?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malnutrition probably to start with. But they had all kinds of infectious diseases including, I mean, the big ones. Luckily, for instance, in that camp-- in Auschwitz-- there were not lice, so that there was very few cases of typhus. But typhoid fever was rampant. I could smell the typhoid. It's got a distinct smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the symptoms of typhoid fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhoid fever? You get a high spiking fever. You get diarrhea. You get spots on your body. You get bloated and you simply die of generalized or edema [INAUDIBLE] swelling-- swelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you commonly know the patients who were in your hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw many friends or family die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name any of those who may have been in your care?

speaker: interviewee
text: Specifically, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The surgeries, Dr. Roden, that you-- you witnessed, which doctors, again, performed them, and who ordered them, and what was their purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was ordered one of the-- by one the SS doctors. And it was done [INAUDIBLE] by one of the three doctors. I don't know exactly which one was on [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, Dr. Mengele. Was he involved in any of these, in the sterilization?

speaker: interviewee
text: He might have ordered-- he actually might have ordered this kind of exploratory laparatomy on these two females. But that's as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had mentioned earlier that the gold teeth needed to be extracted. To whom did you actually bring this bag on a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS men who did the morning appell. And it happened that we had an SS man who was from the northern part of Bohemia-- Sudetenland, S-U-D-E-T-E-N-L-A-N-D-- who spoke Czech. His name was Beztek, B-E-Z-T-E-K. Or D-E-K. T-E-K. And who [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] offered me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to escape [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Auschwitz. The reason being that he found a girl in the camp whom he fell in love with. And he wanted to get her out, but he wanted to test the way first. He explained to me that he was one of the SS-- which I met only a few of them during my stay in the camps-- who was absolutely normal. Quite normal, human-like being, who did not yell, who didn't scream, didn't hit. He also didn't count. He just took the count for granted, and took it with him. And he simply told me, if I want, so-- he cannot tell me exactly how it will be done-- but he needs either money, which he knows we don't have, or gold, which means I have to keep some of the gold teeth back so that it could be melted and he could use it as a bribe to get out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now I was thinking for a couple of days, for he gave me about four days. And I said to myself, for every escapee, the rule in Auschwitz was, if somebody escapes successfully, ten people will die. Meaning if the first one who will die is literally friends and relatives, that means I am automatically sentencing my family to death. And I refused. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Interestingly enough, three weeks later, one of my co-prisoners, whom I knew very well, by the name of Slava, S-L-A-V-A capital L, Lederer, L-E-D-E-R-E-R, escaped with Beztek, successfully. Into the camp walked one SS man. Out of the camp walked two SS men. He brought in a uniform. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They boarded a train in Auschwitz and they went to Prague, with all the documents and everything. And his escape was discovered seven hours later. There was a general absolute panic. And interestingly enough, nothing happened, for it was arranged by an SS man. So that was my [INAUDIBLE] escape. Now we'll go back to Schwarzheide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to Schwarzheide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we are now at the gate. And at the gate of Schwarzheide they are asking-- there are long tables. There are-- there is the SS, about six or eight SS men sitting there. And they are giving us new numbers. We got a new number, which we just had to sew on our striped uniforms. And I mercifully forgot the number. It started with 60-- 65. It was 65,000 something. I forgot the whole number. And they were asking, simply, everybody should report his profession or his occupation. Now, if somebody was silly enough to put down that he's a doctor, for sure he ended with the latrine commander. If he says that he's an engineer, so he would be carrying-- sorry, I have to specify. We were attached as a working commander to a factory, which had 65,000 civilian workers as were manufacturing gasoline, [? Synthing ?] gasoline out of coal. Now we were there to clean after bombardments, for the Allied knew about the camp and they successfully always bombed it in time so that it did-- wasn't producing too much. So if somebody was an engineer, was carrying probably iron rods or bags of cement or architects. And so slowly, there were only about 20 people left. We were workers. And when they asked me what I'm-- my profession of work, so I said I'm a laundryman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They ask me where did I pick up the kind of a trade like laundryman. And so I named the company, Racek-- R-A-C-E-K-- in Prague, which really existed, which I remembered only. And that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you choose to become a laundryman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking, simply, if you are in the camp and you want to stay inside, you don't want to do anything with these huge buildings here. You have to do something which is needed. Now cook, many people would pretend that they are cooks. Some kind of a tailor. Really, they-- they could ask you, I mean, to do something as a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought it was the laundry. Laundry has to be washed from time to time. So I picked up as a laundryman. And to make the story very short, I was given a commando, which varied between 10 people up to 120 people, depending on how much laundry was to be done in the main factory. We didn't wash our laundry. Only we washed the laundry for the civilian workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- it was good for three days a week. And it was a relatively soft job in comparison as anything else. Now we all-- I simply was told I should find people who know something about washing laundry and washing simply clothes, for we-- we-- went to a [INAUDIBLE] laundry, a big laundry. And so we landed there. We have been-- the first time I remember we had been, there were 70. The over-specialist in laundry. I'm sure that nobody has seen a washing machine in his life, or was seen it, but wasn't close. And they were-- this was industrial laundry. I mean, they were machines which were as big as this wall. I mean, five, six meters long, where you had to come on a small track-- rail track and tip the content of the tracks into the thing. And then it goes full and distribute it. it-- you close it automatically. And there was, I mean, all kinds of solution coming through, and it was-- and then there were huge centrifuges where you had to transfer it. And luckily, when we had just came there, so one of the centrifuges got stuck. And one of the guys was an engineer, so he literally crawled into the-- I don't know what he did-- but it was successful in making it work. And that clinched, simply, our knowledge and we were in the laundry. There were six German civilian women there. And now the guy who came with us was the chief commanding [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of a Sergeant Major, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Bleser-- B-L-E-S-E-R. We called him the whip, for that was his specialty was to whip people. And he was quite abrupt. He was from the main camp and he was accustomed to give a speech full of threat. So where we were standing, we were prepared for it already. Like Genghis Khan soldiers in these stripe uniforms, the caps down, and we were-- our hair was cut short and we had here a cross, done with a razor. Yeah. So it looked-- we looked terrific. And he had the speech to us that we are here to work to death, that they will work us to death. And we will work and work and work. But should we just look at one of these German women, immediate death. I mean, he spoke for about five minutes, 10 minutes, and we heard that at least 10 times. And then he made a crucial mistake. He turned towards the six German women and gave them a pep talk, forgetting that they are free German citizens. So that when he left, so they all were trying to get something out of us, for they realized we speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the head of the laundry, her name was Ruhl-- like in the [INAUDIBLE]-- R-U-H-L. And she was telling me-- we are putting sheets through [INAUDIBLE] rollers. She spoke to me, I mean, what-- what are you? Why did we come there [INAUDIBLE]? So I finally said, madam, I cannot talk to you. I mean, I'll be shot. She says, for you to be shot I would have to report you. And I said, who says that you will not report me? She says, I say so. For my two sons who are taken prisoners by the Americans. And if this is what the Americans are doing to them. So I said, madam, I don't know exactly. But I can tell you, every morning, kneel down and pray to your God that they are with the Americans, that they are not with your people. Anyhow, within the next 20 minutes, I let her know about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must say one thing, that from that moment on, nothing was ever reported. And we did awful breaks of rules. And we were getting bread and we were getting margarine and all kinds of stuff from these women. And they would deny everything. And they would always, when some of the key SS men would come with us, so they would take him inside of the laundry and I just got an echo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the thermostat-- a primitive kind of thermostat-- and we raised the heat to the top. We were working half naked, which the SS men liked, but they have been in felt uniform and they lasted about five minutes and they were out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had three free days of-- for this went only for three days. So I became a servant for the leadership-- prisoner leadership of the camp. These were three Germans-- I mean Aryan Germans-- who were in the main camp since 1933. This was their 12th year-- 11th and 12th year in concentration camp. They were imprisoned as Communist. They were straight like a line. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very strict. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were fellow prisoners, and they fought for us tooth and nail. And they, for instance, if somebody would be caught stealing somebody's piece of bread, so they would rip him bad. On the other hand, if the SS men would catch him to do something that he would be hanged, they talked him out of it. And then they were Germans, and they could afford, I mean, to talk to the SS men, like, completely on a different level. And the fact that they lasted 11 years, so the SS men had a certain amount of respect. And they-- really that some of us are here, we have to thank them. And also the march, which would have been otherwise an absolute catastrophe. Many times they saved our skin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for them, Dr. Roden?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cleaned simply the hut where they stayed. They were-- I mean, so naturally they had baths and they had-- each one of them had a bedroom. And I prepared, even, some meal if there was something. And I organized their cigarettes. And I distributed whatever there was in excess to our friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one of them told me, listen, you know really how to do wash? Without kidding, I said, no, by now I know something. He said, you know for, one of the SS men asked me if you couldn't do privately his wash. So the guy comes in a bun-- with a bundle of wash. I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] officer, certainly I can do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I need for that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a container, which would be the best would be to do it out of cement. Then I need a boiler. Then I need something, some wash powder. Preferably, please don't give me any ersatz, for it will dismantle your wash and I will be then at fault. And then I need the washboard. Sorry. And then I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will need some helpers. One can't do it alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, how big should be the container? So I give him the measurements, about one meter by one meter. That was in the morning. By the evening, we had a cement container. We had the-- everything what I named was there. He said, how long will it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The story was that their laundry had to go to the main camp. Before it returned it was six weeks. Now they gave me the laundry in the morning, and in the evening there was, I mean, a tailor, and there was somebody who was ironing this. And he got in there in the evening, completely finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this equipment installed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was installed in our shower room. Where-- where-- where-- where we had our-- where we washed ourself every morning. It was a huge [INAUDIBLE] block, and in a corner we had this kind of facility. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And instead of one, I had four helpers who had absolutely nothing to do but the-- the wash lasted, I don't know. About six, seven SS men were privileged to give us the wash. And this kept them at a very low ebb, for they did something for them. And occasionally, even we got to wash a piece of bread in it. Completely unheard of. It was also-- the year was 1940-- end of 1944, 1945. Also, when somebody was in trouble, so I was in a position that I could say even to this whip, sir, we have something to do with a totally stupid person. I mean, why would you, I mean, let him go. And he would.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use this equipment for any other purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. The day started simply that one was washing-- one was watching what is going on when the commanders already left. And we filled it with nice warm water and took a bath in it. So we washed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had it been since you had been able to take a bath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we could wash our own stuff, and we also had there installed a small pipe stove with the top of it. And when I did the wash, for instance, for the SS men that are responsible for the kitchen. So he gave me the potato peelings and the guys knew that I will be getting it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did something like you can buy nowadays, and we baked them on the top of this stove. And I had around me about 15 guys whom-- who belonged to our group, whom I was feeding this way. Also, when we got something or we organized something, we always divided it into this group. Dr. Roden, you had explained to us about the food rations in Auschwitz. Was it any different in Schwarzheide?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little better. It was a little better in the sense that the bread was-- we-- we got, I don't know-- I don't want to exaggerate-- but something like between 100 and 150 grams of bread. Then we got the soup in the factory. It had really potatoes in it and-- and some kind of meat, probably. I mean, horse meat. But it doesn't make any diff. We were getting a-- a ration of margarine and we were getting a small ration of ersatz marmalade. We had-- and it was about-- the estimate was about 800 to 1,000 calories. One couldn't naturally work and survive it a long time unless one tried to organize something. Like when people were working in the factory, they got something, somewhere, through the civilian workers who took mercy and misery. Also there were French and British prisoners who made sure that the guys will get something. Also we always did something to keep their attention and to be a little bit more merciful. Like we were marching, I remember, to the laundry, and the two SS men which were with us were especially stupid people from Romania, originally. And we were ordered to sing while we are marching. We are marching like a herd of cattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, after you are some remnants of men, march! Sing! Sing. It didn't work. Singing didn't work. So somebody got beaten and they said-- they said to me, can you do something at least? I said, maybe we can whistle. So, OK, whistle. So we are trying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we started to whistle, like one man, 'It's a Long Way to Tipperary.' And the British prisoners and the American prisoners were running towards the street looking and we were totally crazy. And they saw these-- these 100 prisoners simply walking to the laundry room whistling 'It's a Long Way to Tipperary.' That's how we got their attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, we're going to take a break.
